item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 300 meters? Seriously? That's more length than the height of Godzilla!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is an old post but I feel the need to point out the obvious and answer question 4.

Harkin has a deaf brother whom he spoke of extensively as the reason he championed the actual ADA. So it follows that an anomalous version of the act for people with 300 meter fingers would have Harkin instead referring to a brother with 300 meter fingers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of weird I like from the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit:I originally wrote a sort of stream of conciousness rambling thing, I've revised it into a list of issues I have with the article. Not to say I dislike it, I don't, these things just bug mel

1) Three hundred meters is a bit long, its really hard to visualize, and it kinda makes me feel like the author had a poor sense of scale. Three meters alone would have been twice as tall as a person.

2) Aren't most senate hearings televised? I thought the US had a dedicated TV channel just for that. Mind wiping an entire organ of government is a tall order in and of itself, but every one who saw it on TV?

3) the containment procedures strike me as overwrought. Wouldn't it be easier to have the bill amended to cause the buildings currently manifesting the anomaly to cease, or send in agents to make the building slightly non-compliant, instead of tweaking with the elevators or trying to get it repealed? Also, the Foundation isn't a business, so the act shouldn't apply to them anyway, right?

4) former senator Tom Harkin, the person who proposed the bill, doesn't, to my knowledge, have a sibling with arachnodactyly. Is it supposed to be that someone else proposed it, then Harkin proposed the foundation-revised copy? Is Harkin a foundation agent? Does he just think it was his idea? Did the foundation make his brother with arachnodactyly disappear and then erase everyone's memories? Am I just reading too much into this here? This is my weakest issue, to be honest. Wouldnt it make more sense if Harkin proposed it then another Senator tried to add the arachnodactyly rider, triggering foundation involvement?

6) Part of my issue a lack of experimentation when experimentation would be easy and feasible. Register a shell business, make it ADA compliant, prevent civilian access and do your tests there. What happens if you destroy the tubes or damage them? If you punch through the floor or ceiling, what happens? Will a fire on this floor spread to others? Do memos change between visits to reflect foundation activities?

Edited for coherency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's nowhere because the rest of the article already implies their most definitive feature very clearly, so it's not really needed.

if it were actually a thing it would probably just be thousands of boring office memos about office stuff where the fact that they have 300 meter fingers is just casually mentioned here and there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great one, although I can't find the document that contains the descriptions of the employees. It's mentioned on the page but I can't find it anywhere. It would be nice to add it (or maybe it's already there and I'm just too stupid to find it :P).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was sufficiently out there and well-written. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the buttons inside SCP-2354 instances are indented 300 meters relative to the orientation of the button itself. so since the WD-40 is lying sideways and its button pushes downward from the top of the can, it would extend through the can itself.

the little gmod picture i had up on the page before illustrates this nicely, but since that isn't CC compliant i will provide my own visual depiction through my great art skills

click me
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the WD 40 can is laying horizontally how would it extend downward, shouldn't it be vertically?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presumably, SCP-1193 works here.

But the recessess are for long fingers, not long arms. If 1193 tried to use these, he would get his arm stuck in the tubes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Presumably, SCP-1193 works here. By the way: for crosslinking purposes, I would be infinitely tickled if the General Electric headquarters in Fairfield, CT were one of these buildings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not spatially anomalous

Upvoted.

Although, geez, testing these buttons must be incredibly annoying. Three hundred meters is quite a big distance. Also there must be some anomalous tensile strength involved; a 300m tube is going to break under its own weight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly bizarre. I greatly enjoyed the very specific examples, and the absence of explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the author casually sneaked in a mention that the entire legislature was amnesticized. Yeah, we just induced memory loss in all members of the world's most powerful legislative organ. No big deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anomaly itself is fantastically strange, and I love the tie-in with the ADA, both that this bizarre anomaly stems from a piece of legislature, and that the Foundation is considering pushing repeal of a very useful law in order to keep this from spreading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pain for me to really visualize, but I love how fresh and weird it all is. +1 with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has that ovens-and-coffeepot-noses thing going on.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just for the record, the term for "bigger on the inside" geometry is Hyperbolic Space.

The confusion over the name actually goes back to the early 1900's: In Lovecraft's day, non-euclidean geometry did just mean "crazy dimensional hammerspace" (that doesn't follow one or more of the rules of normal euclidean geometry), but they've since formalized and subdivided the field, and now it just refers to curved spaces- elliptical if parallel lines can converge, and hyperbolic if they can diverge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
huh, i didn't know that.

changed it to "spatially anomalous"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still love the idea that the passage of the ADA caused anomalies…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to put things in proper perspective, the likelihood of anyone being sued is vanishingly low. Still, it's good to be familiar with the site's Image Use Policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: alright i dont want this site to get sued so i removed the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. The initial concept and the bit about the Americans with Disabilities Act were pretty neat, although I would have preferred if it were an open question as to whether there really was a guy with 300 m-long fingers running around somewhere. Would have served as a nice piece of fridge logic. I'll second Scan's summation above of "deliciously weird" though. Nicely uncanny.

Two issues. As others have said, the picture probably needs to go, both from compliance issues and the fact that it's obviously computer generated, which breaks immersion for me. I would imagine that it would be possible to achieve a similar effect in photoshop. Also, the Euclidean geometry thing. The regular, non-anomalous world exists in a non-Euclidean setting. Somewhere along the line, "non-Euclidean" became a byword for "crazy dimensional hammerspace," but that's not accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the picture.

pic is ded

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it uses assets from commercial games then it almost certainly isn't compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deliciously weird. I've always had a fondness/fascination with areas of buildings existing where they should not, and every part of this just builds onto the weirdness. The juxtaposition between this anomaly and a three-hour senate speech about disabilities is excellently played. +1.

The picture doesn't really work for me, though. It's distinctly computer-generated (I recognize that texture) and it doesn't contain the underpasses and accessibility features mentioned in the description. It might not be Creative Commons-compliant either, though I can't say for sure.

item.title: first SCP!
item.content:encoded: image made by me, using assets from Black Mesa: Source and Counter-Strike: Source.
